title: Attempts at Translation from Heine
content:
В В В В The Pilgrimage To Kevlaar.

I.

В В В В At the window stood the mother,
В В В В In bed the sick son lay;
В В В В пїЅWill you not get up, William,
В В В В And see them marching away?пїЅ

В В В В пїЅI am so ill, O mother,
В В В В That I cannot hear or see!
В В В В I think of my dead Maggie,
В В В В And my heart is broken in me.пїЅ

В В В В пїЅGet up; we will to Kevlaar,
В В В В Take missal and rosarie;
В В В В The Mother of God our Saviour
В В В В Will heal thy heart for thee.пїЅ

В В В В They wave the broad church-banners,
В В В В They chant the holy song;
В В В В And through Cologne on the Rhine stream,
В В В В The procession draws along.

В В В В The mother follows the pilgrims,
В В В В And her sick son leadeth she;
В В В В And their voices join in the chorale
В В В В пїЅBlessпїЅd be thou, Marie!пїЅ



II.

В В В В The Mother of God at Kevlaar
В В В В To-day wears her richest dress;
В В В В To-day she will be right busy,
В В В В Such numbers come in distress.

В В В В And all the poor sick people
В В В В Bring with them offerings meet;
В В В В They are little waxen figures,
В В В В Many waxen hands and feet.

В В В В And who a wax hand offers,
В В В В His handпїЅs wound hurts no more;
В В В В And who a wax foot offers,
В В В В His foot is healed of its sore.

В В В В To Kevlaar went many on crutches,
В В В В Who now can dance all night;
В В В В And many now play on the viol
В В В В Whose fingers were helpless quite.

В В В В The mother took a wax-light,
В В В В And thereout shaped a heart
В В В В пїЅTake that to our dear LordпїЅs Mother,
В В В В And she will cure thy smart.пїЅ

В В В В Sighing he took the wax heart
В В В В And knelt to the holy form;
В В В В The tears from his eyes outstreaming,
В В В В And the words from his heart blood-warm

В В В В пїЅThou blessпїЅd among women,
В В В В GodпїЅs Virgin pure from taint,
В В В В Thou Queen of the highest Heaven,
В В В В To thee I bring my plaint!

В В В В пїЅI lived with my dear mother
В В В В In the city of Cologne,
В В В В The city for many hundreds
В В В В Of churches and chapels known.

В В В В пїЅAnd next to us lived Maggie,
В В В В She lived, she lives not now
В В В В Marie, I bring thee a wax heart,
В В В В My bleeding heart heal thou!

В В В В пїЅHeal thou my heart sore wounded,
В В В В And early and late to thee
В В В В Will I sing and pray with fervour
В В В В BlessпїЅd be thou, Marie!пїЅ



III.

В В В В The sick son and his mother
В В В В Were sleeping from all ill,
В В В В When lo, the Mother of Jesus
В В В В Came gliding in so still.

В В В В She bent down over the sick one,
В В В В And softly laid her hand
В В В В Upon his heart; then vanished
В В В В Smiling sweet and bland.

В В В В The mother saw all in her dreaming,
В В В В And fain had seen yet more;
В В В В But she was roused from slumber,
В В В В The dogs made such uproar.

В В В В There lay outstretched beside her
В В В В Her son, and he was dead;
В В В В On the pallid features sparkled
В В В В The light of the morning red.

В В В В The mother folded her hands then,
В В В В She felt so wistfully;
В В В В Devoutly sang she softly
В В В В пїЅBlessпїЅd be thou, Marie!пїЅ


В В В В The Loreley.

В В В В I know not what evil is coming,
В В В В But my heart feels sad and cold;
В В В В A song in my head keeps humming,
В В В В A tale from the times of old.

В В В В The air is fresh and it darkles,
В В В В And smoothly flows the Rhine
В В В В The peak of the mountain sparkles
В В В В In the fading sunset-shine.

В В В В The loveliest wonderful Maiden
В В В В On high is sitting there,
В В В В With golden jewels braiden,
В В В В And she combs her golden hair.

В В В В With a golden comb sits combing,
В В В В And ever the while sings she
В В В В A marvellous song through the gloaming
В В В В Of magical melody.

В В В В It hath caught the boatman, and bound him
В В В В In the spell of a wild sad love;
В В В В He sees not the rocks around him,
В В В В He sees only her above.

В В В В The waves through the pass sweep swinging,
В В В В But boatman or boat is none;
В В В В And this with her mighty singing
В В В В The LORELEY hath done.

В В В В The Mountain Voice.
В В В В ALL sadly through the stern ravine rode
В В В В There rode a horseman brave:
В В В В пїЅAh! draw I near to my darlingпїЅs arms,
В В В В Or near to the gloomy grave?пїЅ
В В В В The echo answer gave:
В В В В пїЅTo the gloomy grave!пїЅ

В В В В And as the horseman onward rode
В В В В A deep sigh heaved his breast:
В В В В пїЅIf I thus early go to the grave,
В В В В Well, in the grave is rest!пїЅ
В В В В The answering voice confessed:
В В В В пїЅIn the grave is rest!пїЅ

В В В В Slowly adown the riderпїЅs cheek
В В В В A tear of sad thought fell:
В В В В пїЅIf but in the grave there is rest for me,
В В В В For me in the grave пїЅtis well!пїЅ
В В В В Whereto the echoing knell:
В В В В пїЅIn the grave пїЅtis well!пїЅ

В В В В FOR many thousand ages
В В В В The steadfast stars above
В В В В Have gazed upon each other
В В В В With ever-mournful love.

В В В В They speak a certain language,
В В В В So beautiful, so grand,
В В В В Which none of the philologians
В В В В Could ever understand.

В В В В But I have learned it, learned it
В В В В For ever, by the grace
В В В В Of studying one grammar,
В В В В My heartпїЅs own darlingпїЅs face.

В В В В In the Rhine, in the beautiful river,
В В В В The mighty shadow is thrown,
В В В В With its great cathedral,
В В В В Of holy and great Cologne.

В В В В One picture in the cathedral,
В В В В On gilded leather wrought,
В В В В Unto my lifeпїЅs wild sorrow
В В В В Hath gracious comfort brought:

В В В В The dear Madonna, with floating
В В В В Angels and flowers above;
В В В В The eyes and the lips and the contours
В В В В Are all just those of my love.

В В В В The lotus-flower doth languish
В В В В Beneath the sunпїЅs fierce light;
В В В В With drooping head she waiteth
В В В В All dreamily for night.

В В В В The Moon is her true lover,1
В В В В He wakes her with his glance:
В В В В To him she unveils gladly
В В В В Her gentle countenance.

В В В В She blooms and glows and brightens,
В В В В Intent on him above;
В В В В Exhaling, weeping, trembling,
В В В В With ever-yearning love.

В В В В The world is dull, the world is blind,
В В В В And daily grows more silly!
В В В В It says of you, my lovely child,
В В В В You are not quite a lily.

В В В В The world is dull, the world is blind,
В В В В And judges in stupid fashion
В В В В It knows not how sweet your kisses are,
В В В В And how they burn with passion.

В В В В I BLAME thee not, a broken heart my lot,
В В В В O Love for ever lost! I blame thee not.
В В В В Though thou art splendid with the diamonds bright,
В В В В There falls no gleam within thy heartпїЅs deep night.

В В В В IпїЅve known this long. I saw thee in clear dream,
В В В В And saw black night within thy soul supreme,
В В В В And saw the worm still fretting at thy heart;
В В В В I saw how wretched, O my love, thou art.

В В В В Yes, thou art wretched, and I blame thee not;пїЅ
В В В В My Love, we both must ever wretched be!
В В В В Until deathпїЅs peace concludes our fatal lot,
В В В В My Love, we both must ever wretched be!

В В В В I see the scorn which round thy pale lip weaves,
В В В В And see thine eyes outlighten haughtily,
В В В В And see the pride with which thy bosom heaves;
В В В В And wretched art thou still, wretched as I.

В В В В In secret round thy mouth a pain-thrill steals,
В В В В Through tears held back thine eyes can scarcely see,
В В В В The haughty breast a bleeding heart conceals;
В В В В My Love, we both must ever wretched be.

В В В В The violets blue of the eyes divine,
В В В В And the rose of the cheeks as red as wine,
В В В В And the lilies white of the hands so fine,
В В В В They flourish and flourish from year to year,
В В В В And only the heart is withered and sere.

В В В В The earth is so fair and the heaven so blue,
В В В В And the breeze is breathing so warmly too,
В В В В And the flowers of the meadow are gleaming through
В В В В The sparkling and glittering morning dew,
В В В В And the people are joyous wherever I view:
В В В В Yet would were I in the grave at rest
В В В В Folded close to my lost LoveпїЅs breast.

В В В В I gazed upon her picture,
В В В В Absorbed in dreams of gloom,
В В В В Till those belovпїЅd features
В В В В Began to breathe and bloom.

В В В В About her lips came wreathing
В В В В That sweet, sweet smile I knew;
В В В В The eyes were softly gleaming
В В В В With tears as fresh as dew.

В В В В And my tears sprang then also,
В В В В The dark cloudпїЅs rain was shed:
В В В В And, O my Love, I cannot
В В В В Believe that thou art dead!

В В В В A pine-tree standeth lonely
В В В В In the North on an upland bare;
В В В В It standeth whitely shrouded
В В В В With snow, and sleepeth there:

В В В В It dreameth of a palm-tree,
В В В В Which far in the East alone
В В В В In mournful silence standeth
В В В В On its ridge of burning stone.

В В В В My darling, thou art flowerlike,
В В В В So tender, pure, and fair;
В В В В I gaze on thee, and sadness
В В В В Steals on me unaware:

В В В В I yearn to lay my hands then
В В В В Upon thy head in prayer,
В В В В That God will keep thee ever
В В В В Thus tender, pure, and fair.

В В В В пїЅSay, where is the maiden sweet,
В В В В Whom you once so sweetly sung,
В В В В When the flames of mighty heat
В В В В Filled your heart and fired your tongue?пїЅ

В В В В Ah, those flames no longer burn;
В В В В Cold and drear the heart that fed;
В В В В And this book is but the urn
В В В В Of the ashes of love dead.

В В В В The old dream comes again to me
В В В В With May-night stars above,
В В В В We two sat under the linden-tree
В В В В And swore eternal love.

В В В В Again and again we plighted troth,
В В В В We chattered, and laughed, and kissed;
В В В В To make me well remember my oath
В В В В You gave me a bite in the wrist.

В В В В O darling with the eyes serene,
В В В В And with the teeth so white!
В В В В The vows were proper to the scene,
В В В В Superfluous was the bite.

В В В В My darling, we sat together,
В В В В We two in our frail boat;
В В В В The night was calm oпїЅer the wide sea
В В В В Whereon we were afloat.

В В В В The Spectre-Island, the lovely,
В В В В Lay dim in the moonпїЅs mild glance;
В В В В There sounded sweetest music,
В В В В There waved the shadowy dance.

В В В В It sounded sweet and sweeter,
В В В В It waved there to and fro;
В В В В But we slid past forlornly
В В В В Upon the great sea-flow.

В В В В My heart, my heart is mournful,
В В В В Yet joyously shines the May;
В В В В I stand by the linden leaning,
В В В В High on the bastion grey.

В В В В The blue town-moat thereunder
В В В В Glides peacefully along;
В В В В A boy in a boat is angling
В В В В And whistling a careless song.

В В В В Beyond, like a well-known picture,
В В В В All small and fair are strewed
В В В В Houses and gardens and people,
В В В В Oxen and meadows and wood.

В В В В The maidens bleach the linen,
В В В В And dance in the grass for glee;
В В В В The mill-wheel scatters diamonds,
В В В В Its far hum reaches me.

В В В В Upon the hoary tower
В В В В A sentry-box stands low;
В В В В A youth in his coat of scarlet
В В В В There paces to and fro.

В В В В He trifles with his musket,
В В В В Which gleams in the sunshine red;
В В В В He shoulders and presents itпїЅ
В В В В I would he shot me dead.



В В В В QUESTIONS.

В В В В By the sea, by the desert midnight sea,
В В В В Stands a youth,
В В В В His heart full of anguish, his head full of doubt,
В В В В And with sullen lips he questions the waves:пїЅ

В В В В пїЅOh, Oh, solve to me the Riddle of Life,
В В В В The painful primordial riddle,
В В В В Which already has racked so many heads,
В В В В Heads in hieroglyphic caps,
В В В В Heads in turbans and black berrets,
В В В В Heads in wigs, and myriad other
В В В В Poor perspiring human heads;
В В В В What is the meaning of Man?
В В В В Whence comes he? Whither goes he?
В В В В Who dwells there above in the golden stars?пїЅ

В В В В The waves murmur their everlasting murmur,
В В В В The wind sweeps, the clouds scud,
В В В В The stars glitter indifferent and cold,
В В В В And, a fool awaits an answer.

В В В В As I each day in the morning
В В В В Pass by that house of thine,
В В В В It gives me joy, thou darling,
В В В В When you at the window shine.

В В В В Your dark brown eyes they ask me,
В В В В As only sweet eyes can
В В В В Who art thou, and what ails thee,
В В В В Thou sickly foreign man?

В В В В I am a German poet,
В В В В Well known beyond the Rhine;
В В В В When men the best names mention,
В В В В Be sure they mention mine.

В В В В And what ails me, thou darling,
В В В В Ails many beyond the Rhine;
В В В В When men the worst woes mention,2
В В В В Be sure they mention mine.

В В В В You lovely fisher-maiden,
В В В В Bring now the boat to land:
В В В В Come here and sit beside me,
В В В В WeпїЅll prattle hand in hand.

В В В В Your head lay on my bosom,
В В В В Nor be afraid of me:
В В В В Do you not trust all fearless
В В В В Daily the great wild sea?

В В В В My heart is like the sea, dear,
В В В В Has storm, and ebb, and flow,
В В В В And many purest pearl-gems
В В В В Within its dim depth glow.

В В В В The moon is fully risen,
В В В В And shineth over the sea;
В В В В And I embrace my darling,
В В В В Our hearts swell free.

В В В В In the arms of the lovely maiden
В В В В I lie alone on the strand:
В В В В пїЅWhat sounds in the breezeпїЅs sighing?
В В В В Why trembles your white hand?пїЅ

В В В В пїЅThat is no breezeпїЅs sighing,
В В В В That is the mermaidensпїЅ song,
В В В В The singing of my sisters
В В В В Whom the sea hath drowned so long.пїЅ

В В В В Where?

В В В В Where shall once the wanderer weary
В В В В Meet his resting-place and shrine?
В В В В Under palm-trees by the Ganges?
В В В В Under lindens of the Rhine?

В В В В Shall I somewhere in the desert
В В В В Owe my grave to stranger hands?
В В В В Or upon some lonely sea-shore
В В В В Rest at last beneath the sands?

В В В В Ever onward! GodпїЅs wide heaven
В В В В Must surround me there as here;
В В В В And like death-lamps oпїЅer me swinging
В В В В Night by night the stars burn clear.



В В В В Body and Soul.

В В В В The poor Soul speaketh to its Clay
В В В В I cannot leave thee thus; IпїЅll stay
В В В В With thee, with thee in death will sink
В В В В And black Annihilation drink.
В В В В Thou still hast been my second I,
В В В В Embracing me so lovingly;
В В В В A satin feast-robe round my form
В В В В Doubled with ermine soft and warm.
В В В В WoeпїЅs me! I dare not face the factпїЅ
В В В В Quite disembodied, quite abstract,
В В В В To loiter as a blessпїЅd Naught
В В В В Above there in the realm of Thought,
В В В В Through Heavenly halls immense and frigid,
В В В В Where the Immortals dumb and rigid
В В В В Yawn to me as they clatter by
В В В В With leaden clogs so wearily.
В В В В Oh, it is horrible! Oh, stay,
В В В В Stay with me, thou beloved Clay!

В В В В The Body to the poor Soul said:
В В В В Oh, murmur not, be comforted!
В В В В We all should quietly endure
В В В В The wounds of Fate, which none can cure.
В В В В I was the lampпїЅs wick, and to dust
В В В В Consume; but thou, the Spirit, must
В В В В Be saved with care, and lifted far
В В В В To shine in Heaven, a little star
В В В В Of purest light. I am but cinder,
В В В В Mere matter, rubbish, rotten tinder,
В В В В Losing the shape we took at birth,
В В В В Mouldering again to earth in earth.
В В В В Now, fare thee well, and grieve no more!
В В В В Perchance life is not such a bore
В В В В In Heaven, as you-expect up there.
В В В В If you should meet the old Great Bear
В В В В (Not Meyer-Bear3) iпїЅ the starry climes,
В В В В Greet him from me a thousand times!
meaning: About: James Thomson (November 23, 1834 - June 3, 1882), published under the pseudonym Bysshe Vanolis, was a Victorian-era British poet famous primarily for the long poem The City of Dreadful Night (1874), an expression of bleak pessimism in a dehumanized, uncaring urban environment.
